title: Mira
description: A vision-first AI reading assistant that acts like a "Cursor for textbooks"—understands pages in real time and provides contextual explanations with minimal friction.

writeup.architecture: Instead of traditional RAG pipelines, Mira uses a vision-first approach by sending page screenshots directly to a multimodal LLM. Each PDF page maintains its own isolated conversation context, effectively creating per-page memory threads. The system routes queries either to page-level reasoning (via screenshots) or document-level reasoning (by sending the full PDF) depending on user intent.
writeup.implementation: Built as a PDF reader with an embedded chat interface. On each query, the app captures the current page as an image and sends it along with prior page-specific chat history. Responses are post-processed into smaller message chunks to mimic natural conversation. Optional text-to-speech is integrated via Kokoro (hosted on Replicate), converting LLM outputs into audio. The system also optimizes token usage by avoiding text extraction and relying solely on image inputs.
writeup.results: Eliminated the need for complex PDF parsing while improving contextual understanding of diagrams, equations, and layouts. Reduced token usage (~250 tokens per page image) compared to text+image pipelines. Delivered a significantly smoother UX by removing friction from context switching and transforming long LLM outputs into conversational responses. Demonstrated that vision-based approaches can simplify architecture while maintaining strong accuracy for educational use cases.

title: gql-codegen
description: Generates fully type-safe TypeScript clients from GraphQL schemas. Zero runtime overhead — all types resolved at build time.

writeup.architecture: Parses GraphQL schema files using the reference graphql-js parser, walks the AST to extract type and field definitions, and emits TypeScript interfaces that mirror the schema exactly. A watch mode re-runs on schema changes.
writeup.implementation: Maps GraphQL scalar types to TypeScript primitives, handles nullable vs. non-null correctly, and supports custom scalar mappings via config. Output is fully deterministic for clean diffs in code review.
writeup.results: Eliminated a class of runtime type errors where GraphQL response shapes diverged from TypeScript interfaces. Generates types for schemas with 200+ types in under 100ms.

title: oxidize
description: Zero-copy binary protocol parser for Rust. Leverages the type system to guarantee memory safety without sacrificing performance.

writeup.architecture: Parsing without allocation requires borrowing from the input buffer for the lifetime of the parsed value. The library models this with Rust lifetime parameters, so the compiler statically verifies that the input outlives any value derived from it.
writeup.implementation: Provides a derive macro that generates a Parser impl for any struct with borrowed fields. Fixed-width fields are parsed as big-endian integers; variable-length fields use a length prefix read earlier in the frame. No unsafe code in user-facing APIs.
writeup.results: Zero heap allocations on the hot path. Parses a 64-byte binary frame in ~12ns on an M2, compared to ~180ns for an equivalent approach using Vec<u8>.

title: tensors
description: Lightweight Python tensor library with NumPy-compatible API, written from scratch to understand autodiff and backpropagation.

writeup.architecture: Each Tensor wraps a NumPy array and carries a closure that computes the gradient contribution to its inputs. The forward pass builds an implicit computation graph; calling .backward() traverses it in reverse topological order, accumulating gradients.
writeup.implementation: Pure Python with NumPy for array operations. Implements +, *, matmul, relu, and sigmoid with correct gradient formulas. Supports batched operations — gradient accumulation handles broadcasting automatically.
writeup.results: Trains a two-layer MLP on MNIST to 97.4% accuracy in ~60 seconds on CPU. Matches PyTorch output on identical network architectures and learning rates.
